transcript.text: Gas chambers in crematorium 4 where thousands of Jewish men, women, and children were murdered by gas and their bodies burned. The crematorium was also used for disposal of bodies of prisoners of the concentration camp, both Jews and non-Jews, who had died from other causes.
The gas chambers in crematorium 4 operated intermittently from March 43 to October 7, 1944. On that day, Jewish prisoners of the Sonderkommando revolted, set fire to the building, and destroyed it.
And that's what it looked like. And that's what it looks like today.
We have some more buildings over there. There's the central part of Auschwitz, off in the distance. Very big place.
Those are the sewage plants. That's where I came from.
There's a tower way off in the distance. That's where I came from.
Here's the gas chamber crematorium.
Here's where they were gassed. This area right here. And this area on the end right here.
There were lots of gas chambers here. These were some of the newer ones.
Of course, the fences around the grounds and the white tower.
Here's one of the Auschwitz building's entrances. I don't know what that is. Auschwitz church or something.
That's where I was just at the crematorium. That's where I came from and where I'm going now.
Newly arrived prisoners had to undress in this room. Clothing, valuables, and documents belonging to non-Jewish prisoners were taken for deposit in the camp storage rooms.
Property belonging to Jews, whether they were murdered on arrival or assigned to labor in the camp, was confiscated and taken to nearby warehouse area known as Canada.
After sorting and disinfection, I was shipped to various destinations in Germany, mainly for the use of German civilians.
Here's the room. Rooms of Auschwitz.
These are valuables. Real valuables. Real possessions of Jews.
These are the clothes that were deloused in these chambers. Today we decided to strip their clothes in this corridor.
And there's a delousing chambers right here. Maybe even crematoria.
They marched toward their own.
In order to make prisoners conspicuous and to prevent escape, all their hair was cut off.
This is the room where the camp barbers cut and shaved all their hair from their bodies of male and female prisoners.
They did so with blunt instruments and at great speed the prisoners were offered injured.
The procedure was particularly humiliating for women since the barbers were often men and they were shaved in the presence of jeering SS men.
The tattooing off serial numbers assigned to prisoners as part of the registration procedure was sometimes done here.
The numbers replaced their names.
So this is the room where they did that.
And then they were sent.
It says,
Unter Suchenstraum.
And then they went into another room.
To the Bradsen.
Prisoners showered and were disinfected in this room.
They were frequently harassed in various ways.
Several hundred people at a time were crowded in here on water that was usually ice cold or scalding hot poured onto them from the shower heads and the ceiling.
Prisoners usually received neither soap nor towels.
After the shower they went to the next room where they were supposed to dry off.
Those are real prisoners being led away from the shower.
This is the shower room.
With the drainages there.
They don't have the shower heads anymore.
These are the room where they were supposed to dry off.
Prisoners were assembled in this room after their shower naked and wet standing or sitting on the concrete floor and exposed to drafts.
They waited as long as several hours for their distribution of special camp clothing.
Eventually they were driven down the corridor to the room where they were given camp clothes.
And before entering this room they had to wait as much as seven hours naked or standing regardless of weather.
Or as much as twelve hours.
I guess these are office rooms.
Then they went under this corridor.
There's a disinfection.
Delousing chambers.
Here's photos of the delousing chambers.
Marking on them after getting their clothing.
Prisoners operating the equipment in which clothing was disinfected by means of steam down there.
Prisoners operating the chambers in which clothing was disinfected by hot air.
So there's a lot of disinfecting floors.
There are photos found of victims.
The cart used to haul the dead bodies to be cremated.
There is gas chamber one.
Major gas chamber two where hundreds of thousands of Jews were killed in each one.
And there's the grounds of Berknew concentration camp at Auschwitz.
There's a cart probably for carrying bodies.
Here's where I came from.
Where I'm going.
So here's an aerial photo of the Berknew concentration camps.
All the little dots are these buildings here.
Today is Auschwitz I.
That's where I did the first tour.
This big area here is Auschwitz II.
That's where I've just been walking around.
I was riding my bike down here.
I didn't even know Auschwitz was here.
I got to here and I saw the sign Auschwitz.
I was like what?
Okay.
Check it out.
It's a very big area.
That is the town Auschwitz in the name.
I don't know how you pronounce it in Polish.
But the whole town is not even as big as the Auschwitz II camp.
Very big.
Very big.
